hero_desc: I build interactive experiences with C#, Unity and shaders. Specialized in NPC AI, gameplay systems and real-time visual effects.
about_p1: I'm a Unity Developer with experience in gameplay mechanics using C#: movement, 3D physics, collisions and the new Input System. My background as a Systems Technician gives me a solid foundation in problem-solving and structured logic.
about_p2: I specialize in NPC AI and behavior —enemies with patrolling, chasing, visual/audio detection and unique reactions— and in shader visual effects: auras, fake 3D lights, selective transparency and post-processing. I've participated in 36 and 72-hour game jams, and my projects are published on itch.io.
about_p3: Currently I manage Git, work in multidisciplinary teams and continue training in optimization and data persistence.
tept_desc: 3D tower defense with day/night cycle. Adaptive AI, data persistence, cinematics and custom shaders.
ienemies_desc: 36-hour game jam. Enemy with 3 states: patrolling, fleeing and "chill". Dynamic lights with real-time shadows.
nightlight_desc: 2D stealth game. A creature stalks you in the dark. Find the key and escape using your flashlight and inventory.
soplos_desc: My first game jam. Main mechanics implementation in Construct 3. Shared AI correction and collisions.
vfx_intro: Shaders and visual effects developed for my Unity projects. Each one represents a different technical challenge in real-time rendering.
vfx_aura_desc: Pulsating aura effect on enemies using vertex displacement and color gradient with Fresnel.
vfx_light_desc: Fake volumetric lighting generated with rotated planes that simulate 3D light beams without real lighting cost.
vfx_transparency_desc: Shader that makes structures transparent when they obstruct the player's view using dithering.
vfx_camera_desc: Post-processing that simulates a surveillance camera: intense bloom, dark vignette and grain.
edu_adso: Technologist in Software Analysis and Development
contact_desc: Do you have a project in mind, a job opportunity or simply want to chat about game development? Write me!
footer_text: © 2026 Sebastián Franco Gomez — Built with and clean code
tept_about_p1: T.E.P.T 1945 is a 3D tower defense set in World War II with a dynamic day and night cycle. Enemies have adaptive behaviors that change depending on the time of day: during the day they attack the player's defenses, and at night they chase the player directly.
tept_about_p2: It was my bootcamp final project, where I integrated everything I learned: custom shaders, AI with differentiated behaviors, data persistence with PlayerPrefs, cinematics with Cinemachine and a progressive wave system.
tept_felt: This project was the most ambitious I've developed so far. I felt great satisfaction seeing how the shaders I created from scratch (the enemy aura, the fake 3D light and selective transparency) gave the game a unique visual identity. The biggest challenge was making the AI feel organic both day and night, and that data persistence worked correctly to save the player's progress.
tept_tech_1: Adaptive AI: During the day, enemies follow pathfinding toward the towers to attack them. At night, they switch to direct chase mode against the player with visual detection using raycasts.
tept_tech_2: Aura shader: Pulsating effect on enemies using vertex displacement and color gradient with Fresnel effect in Shader Graph.
tept_tech_3: Fake 3D light shader: Volumetric lighting simulation using rotated planes with transparency, without the need for real lights (performance optimization).
tept_tech_4: Selective transparency shader: When an object obstructs the camera between the player and the action, the shader applies dithering to make it semi-transparent.
tept_tech_5: Data persistence: Save system with PlayerPrefs for player progress (level reached, accumulated resources, settings).
tept_tech_6: Cinematics: Sequences with Cinemachine + Timeline for the introduction and day/night transitions.
tept_gallery_soon: Coming soon: Additional GIFs of the aura shader, fake 3D light and selective transparency in action.
ienemies_about: I have no enemies was born in a 36-hour game jam. The challenge was to create an enemy with unconventional behaviors: instead of simply chasing the player, the enemy flees with a key object when it detects the player and has unique behavior states.
ienemies_about_2: I took the lead role in AI programming and interaction systems. The team was small but focused, which allowed for very fast iteration.
ienemies_felt: Very proud of having achieved that the enemy had differentiated and coherent behaviors in such a short time. The most satisfying moment was seeing how the enemy detected the player, grabbed the cat and fled in a believable way.
ienemies_tech_1: State machine: 3 modes — Patrol (base movement), Flee (detects the player with raycast, grabs the cat and moves away) and Chill (AI disabled).
ienemies_tech_2: Visual detection with Raycast: 2D raycasts to detect the player in its field of view.
ienemies_tech_3: Dynamic lights system: Lights that turn on when passing nearby and turn off afterwards, with real-time shadows.
ienemies_tech_4: Key object (cat): The enemy grabs and flees with it; the player must prevent it.
nightlight_about: Nightlight is a 2D top-down stealth game. You wake up at night and a creature stalks you. You must find the key to escape using your flashlight with limited battery and an inventory with weight logic.
nightlight_about_2: Born in a 72-hour game jam. I was the lead programmer (~65% of the mechanics).
nightlight_felt: Very satisfied. Seeing how the AI reacted to the player —patrolling, detecting, hunting— and how the weight system affected the experience gave me a lot of confidence.
nightlight_tech_1: Enemy AI: State machine with patrolling, visual/audio detection, searching and hunting.
nightlight_tech_2: Flashlight system: Directional with limited battery that attracts the enemy.
nightlight_tech_3: Inventory: 1 item, with pickup, inspection and use.
nightlight_tech_4: Weight logic: Affects player speed. Puzzle: door requires weight threshold.
nightlight_tech_5: Menus: Pause, game over and victory integrated.
soplos_about: Breaths of Life was my first game jam and my first real approach to development. We used Construct 3 (no-code engine, based on event sheets). It was a key formative experience.
soplos_felt: Intense and revealing. The most satisfying moment was fixing the "hive mind" bug where all enemies shared AI. Making them independent taught me the importance of clean architecture.
soplos_tech_1: Movement and pursuit: Visual detection system with Construct 3 event sheet.
soplos_tech_2: Critical AI fix: I made each enemy independent with separate instances.
soplos_tech_3: Testing and debugging: Exhaustive validation of main mechanics.
